Sample Digest · Monday 27 April 2026

A recent Emergency Medicine digest,
as sent to subscribers

This is the real Monday digest from Monday 27 April 2026. Showing the first 8 of 29 articles from that week — every new paper from the top journals in Emergency Medicine.

29articles that week
4journals covered
£0for your first 4 weeks
Note: Summaries are AI-generated from publicly available PubMed abstracts. Always verify against the source article before clinical use.
Academic emergency medicine : official journal of the Society for Academic Emergency Medicine

Multi-Factorial and Multi-Component Fall Prevention Interventions Initiated From the Emergency Department: A Systemic Review and Meta-Analysis

Southerland LT, Mowbray FI, Tarnovsky IA et al. · 2026 Apr
Study Type: Systematic review and meta-analysis
Key Question: Do multi-factorial and multi-component fall prevention interventions initiated from the emergency department reduce falls in older adults aged ≥60 years?
Key Findings:
  • No reduction in fall risk at 3, 6, or 12 months (risk differences ranging from -0.02 to 0.07, with confidence intervals crossing null)
  • No change in ED revisits at 1, 3, or 12 months, nor in mortality or hospitalisation rates
  • Functional status improved in 4 of 5 studies that measured this outcome
Clinical Relevance: Despite national guideline endorsement, ED-initiated comprehensive fall prevention programmes do not appear to reduce falls or healthcare utilisation in older adults, challenging current UK emergency medicine practice recommendations.
Limitations: High heterogeneity in intervention types, settings, and outcome timing limits the ability to draw definitive conclusions about specific intervention components.
PubMed →DOI →
Academic emergency medicine : official journal of the Society for Academic Emergency Medicine

Preemptive Anticoagulation for Patients With Suspected Pulmonary Embolism in the Emergency Department: An International Survey of Emergency Physicians

Grewal K, Stubblefield WB, Casey SD et al. · 2026 Apr
Study Type: International survey
Key Question: How frequently do emergency physicians use preemptive anticoagulation for suspected pulmonary embolism patients awaiting imaging, and what factors influence this practice?
Key Findings:
  • Only 76.8% of 413 emergency physicians from 13 countries sometimes or always use preemptive anticoagulation, with 23.1% never using it
  • Over two-thirds were unaware of guideline recommendations, and half lacked institutional protocols
  • Clinical experience (OR 1.81, 95% CI 1.38-2.38), higher PE knowledge (OR 2.05, 95% CI 1.03-4.06), and concern for cardiovascular deterioration (OR 3.21, 95% CI 1.88-5.49) increased likelihood of use
Clinical Relevance: This identifies significant knowledge gaps and protocol deficiencies that may lead to underuse of evidence-based preemptive anticoagulation in UK EDs, potentially affecting patient outcomes for suspected PE cases.
Limitations: Survey responses may not reflect actual clinical practice, and the 27.6% response rate introduces potential selection bias.
PubMed →DOI →
Academic emergency medicine : official journal of the Society for Academic Emergency Medicine

The Downstream Effects of Prehospital Opioid Dosing in Older Adults: A Retrospective Cohort Study

McGuire SS, Brown CS, Palmer AK et al. · 2026 Apr
Study Type: Retrospective cohort study
Key Question: Does prehospital opioid dosing in older adults (≥65 years) affect emergency department and hospital outcomes?
Key Findings:
  • High-dose prehospital opioids (versus low/therapeutic doses) increased risk of altered responsiveness by 28% (aRR 1.28, 95% CI: 1.06-1.56) and delirium by 165% (aRR 2.65, 95% CI: 1.25-5.59)
  • No differences observed in hypoxia rates, 30-day mortality, or ED length of stay between dosing groups
  • Of 6,406 older patients receiving prehospital opioids, 11.7% received high doses, 57.2% therapeutic doses, and 31.2% low doses
Clinical Relevance: UK emergency clinicians should be aware that older patients receiving high-dose prehospital opioids may present with increased cognitive impairment, potentially complicating assessment and disposition decisions.
Limitations: Single health system study limits generalisability, and retrospective design cannot establish causation between opioid dosing and observed outcomes.
PubMed →DOI →
Academic emergency medicine : official journal of the Society for Academic Emergency Medicine

Association of Early Steroid and Antibiotic Therapy With Airway Outcomes in Adult Epiglottitis: A 10-Year Multicenter Retrospective Cohort Study

O'Brien J, Muccio DR, Schrock JW · 2026 Apr
Study Type: Retrospective cohort study
Key Question: Does early corticosteroid or antibiotic therapy reduce airway complications in adults presenting with epiglottitis?
Key Findings:
  • Early corticosteroids reduced intubation risk by 52% (RR 0.48, 95% CI 0.26-0.88) and ICU admission by 29% (RR 0.71, 95% CI 0.55-0.92)
  • Early antibiotics similarly reduced intubation risk by 46% (RR 0.54, 95% CI 0.34-0.85) and ICU admission by 28% (RR 0.72, 95% CI 0.60-0.86)
  • Adult epiglottitis incidence increased 2.5-fold over the decade, with steepest rise in 60-74 year age group
Clinical Relevance: This supports early aggressive medical therapy with both steroids and antibiotics for adult epiglottitis in UK EDs, potentially reducing airway interventions and critical care admissions.
Limitations: Retrospective design using administrative codes may introduce selection bias and misclassification of epiglottitis cases.
PubMed →DOI →
Emergency medicine journal : EMJ

Preinjury, injury and postinjury factors leading to death in children and young people who were victims of knife crime in England between 2019 and 2024: a review of the National Child Mortality Database

Roberts T, Odd D, Coveney J et al. · 2026 Apr 20
Study Type: Retrospective cohort study
Key Question: What are the demographic, pre-injury, injury, and post-injury factors associated with knife-related deaths in children and young people under 18 years in England?
Key Findings:
  • 145 children died from knife wounds (2019-2024); 90.3% male, mean age 14.4 years, with Black/Black British children having 13-fold higher death rates (IRR 13.29, 95% CI 8.23-20.00) than White children
  • Children in most deprived areas had >7-fold higher risk (IRR 7.48, 95% CI 3.22-17.29); 60.3% died before hospital arrival, with chest/neck injuries causing 75.9% of deaths
  • 75.4% were known to social services, 57.9% had experienced domestic violence, and 50.9% had neurodiversity/mental health concerns
Clinical Relevance: Emergency clinicians should recognise that knife injury victims often have complex social backgrounds and high pre-hospital mortality, informing both immediate trauma management priorities and broader safeguarding awareness.
Limitations: Only 57 of 145 cases were available for detailed analysis, potentially limiting the generalisability of injury pattern and social factor findings.
PubMed →DOI →
Emergency medicine journal : EMJ

Absorbable sutures appear clinically effective and may be more cost-effective than non-absorbable sutures for definitive closure of adult facial lacerations

Kelly M, Horner DE · 2026 Apr 22
Study Type: Short cut review (rapid evidence synthesis)
Key Question: Are absorbable sutures as effective as non-absorbable sutures for definitive closure of adult facial lacerations in the emergency department?
Key Findings:
  • Absorbable sutures deliver comparable cosmetic outcomes to non-absorbable sutures for facial laceration closure
  • No difference in complication rates between absorbable and non-absorbable sutures
  • Absorbable sutures may be more cost-effective by eliminating need for suture removal appointments
Clinical Relevance: This supports using absorbable sutures for facial lacerations in UK EDs, potentially reducing patient follow-up burden and healthcare costs while maintaining clinical outcomes.
Limitations: Based on only two suitable papers identified through the search strategy, limiting the strength of evidence synthesis.
PubMed →DOI →
Emergency medicine journal : EMJ

Management and outcomes of critically ill patients admitted to a critical care resuscitation unit: a retrospective cohort study

Noitz M, Jenny D, Steininger F et al. · 2026 Apr 24
Study Type: Retrospective cohort study
Key Question: What are the management patterns and outcomes of critically ill patients treated in a critical care resuscitation unit (CCRU) compared to standard emergency department care?
Key Findings:
  • Of 2059 CCRU patients, 44.2% required ICU admission after median 2.7 hours stay, while 47.5% were stepped down to general wards and 4.1% died in CCRU
  • CCRU implementation reduced ICU admission rates for ED patients from 0.73% to 0.56% (p<0.001) and shortened time to critical care initiation from 1.87 to 0.85 hours (p<0.001)
  • Patients received median 2 critical care interventions during their CCRU stay
Clinical Relevance: CCRUs may offer UK emergency departments a model for early critical care intervention that reduces ICU demand while maintaining patient safety—relevant given NHS capacity pressures and four-hour targets.
Limitations: Historical control comparison limits causal inference due to potential confounding factors and temporal changes in practice patterns.
PubMed →DOI →
Emergency medicine journal : EMJ

External validation of the emergency CT head score to reduce non-trauma imaging: a multicentre retrospective study

Seingier R, Pasco J, Hanouz JL et al. · 2026 Apr 24
Study Type: Multicentre retrospective validation study
Key Question: Can the Emergency CT Head Score (ECHS) safely reduce unnecessary non-trauma head CT scans in French emergency departments?
Key Findings:
  • ECHS showed excellent discriminatory ability (AUC 0.894, 95% CI 0.849-0.940) in 600 adults presenting with non-traumatic neurological symptoms
  • Using ECHS≥1 threshold achieved 100% sensitivity (95% CI 95.8-100%) and 100% negative predictive value (95% CI 98.2-100%) with no missed abnormalities
  • 48% of head CT scans could potentially be avoided in patients scoring ECHS=0 without adverse outcomes
Clinical Relevance: This validates a simple 4-criteria scoring tool that could significantly reduce unnecessary head CT imaging in UK EDs for patients presenting with non-traumatic neurological symptoms, addressing resource pressures and radiation exposure concerns.
Limitations: Retrospective design without prospective validation or clinical follow-up to confirm safety of withholding scans in ECHS=0 patients.
PubMed →DOI →
